situation.content:
Your favourite team lead got promoted to director last year. She used to eat lunch with the team, stay late to pair on hard problems, fight for everyone's ideas in planning. Now she speaks in strategy jargon, cancels 1-on-1s, and seems to care more about her VP's opinion than the team's reality.

She didn't become a bad person. Something more unsettling happened — the role became her.

story.content:
In August 1971, psychologist Philip Zimbardo converted the basement of Stanford's psychology building into a mock prison. He recruited 24 college students — screened to be psychologically normal, healthy, and without criminal history — and randomly assigned them as either guards or prisoners. The experiment was supposed to last two weeks.

It was terminated after six days.

Within 36 hours, the guards — ordinary students who days earlier had been playing frisbee on the quad — began displaying authoritarian behavior. They forced prisoners to do push-ups, stripped them of names (replacing them with numbers), and woke them for arbitrary 2am counts. By day three, one-third of the guards showed genuinely sadistic tendencies. They weren't following a script. No one told them to be cruel. The role created the behavior.

The prisoners, equally ordinary students, broke down even faster. Within 48 hours, the first prisoner had to be released due to acute emotional disturbance. Others stopped resisting entirely — becoming passive, depressed, displaying signs of learned helplessness. They had internalized "prisoner" as identity, not assignment.

Here's what matters for understanding promotions: none of these students were predisposed to cruelty or submission. The random assignment determined everything. Same people, different role, different behavior. The role didn't reveal character — it created it.

Zimbardo later called this the "Lucifer Effect" — the tendency for situational forces, especially hierarchical position, to reshape behavior more powerfully than individual personality. The guards didn't think: "I'm going to be cruel today." They thought: "This is what being a guard means. This is what the role requires." Position creates its own logic, its own psychology, its own version of who you should be.

The same mechanism operates in every promotion. The new director doesn't decide to become distant. She absorbs what "director" means from context cues: other directors don't eat lunch with ICs, directors speak in strategy language, directors cancel meetings when the VP needs them. The role rewrites behavior not through conscious choice but through environmental pressure — and it happens so gradually that the person rarely notices they've changed.
story.title: The Story

contrast.content:
In 458 BC, the Roman Senate granted Cincinnatus absolute dictatorial power to defend Rome from an invading army. He was a farmer — called from his plow to save the republic. He took command, defeated the enemy in sixteen days, and immediately resigned the dictatorship, returning to his farm. The entire event took three weeks.

Cincinnatus is remembered precisely because he's the exception. Power didn't change him because he had an identity anchored outside the role — he was a farmer who happened to be commanding, not a commander who happened to farm. The role remained something he wore, not something he became. This required an identity strong enough to resist the gravitational pull of position — and two thousand years of celebration tells us how rare that is.
contrast.title: The Contrast

principle.content:
Position doesn't reveal character — it creates it. The role rewrites behavior through environmental pressure, not conscious choice.

Promotions change people not because power "corrupts" (implying a moral failing) but because position restructures incentives, information flows, social pressure, and identity — all below conscious awareness. The question isn't "will the role change me?" — it's "which parts of me do I want to anchor before the role starts pulling?"

psychology.content:
Social role theory (Eagly, 1987) shows that people adopt the behaviors expected of their roles with remarkable speed and thoroughness. The mechanism is social reinforcement: act like others in your position, receive validation. Act unlike them, receive friction. Within weeks, the path of least resistance reshapes behavior.

The power literature adds a critical finding: elevated power reduces perspective-taking (Galinsky et al., 2006). People in powerful positions literally become worse at reading others' emotions and considering others' viewpoints — not because they choose to, but because the neural pathways for perspective-taking are suppressed when the brain registers hierarchical advantage. Power physiologically changes the brain.

This explains why promoted people seem to "lose empathy" — they often haven't lost the capacity, but the positional signal dampens its activation. Add to this that promoted people receive less honest feedback (subordinates filter upward communication), and you get a feedback-blind environment where the person can't even see that they've changed.

The Zimbardo finding that bothers psychologists most: personality inventories taken before the experiment had no predictive power. Who became sadistic and who remained humane was not predicted by who they were before. It was predicted by situational factors in the moment.
psychology.title: Why It Happens

move.content:
1. Anchor your identity before the role pulls. Cincinnatus survived power because "farmer" was his identity, not "dictator." Before or immediately after a promotion, write down: Who am I independent of this title? What behaviors do I commit to keeping regardless of positional pressure? Review quarterly.

2. Build feedback channels that survive hierarchy. Power degrades perspective-taking AND makes honest feedback less likely. Explicitly create channels that bypass the hierarchy: skip-levels, anonymous feedback, a trusted peer who will tell you when you've changed. If you have to ask "have I changed?" you've already started.

3. Maintain practices from your previous level. Eat lunch with the team. Pair on problems occasionally. Keep one 1-on-1 that you never cancel. These aren't just nice gestures — they're identity anchors that resist the role's gravitational pull toward distance.

4. Watch for the jargon shift. When you start speaking in the language of your new level ("alignment," "strategy," "leverage") without noticing, the role is rewriting your communication patterns. Jargon isn't just language — it's a signal that you've absorbed the identity. Notice it.

5. Remember: the role creates behavior, not reveals it. When you see a promoted colleague change, resist the narrative that "power showed who they really are." It probably created who they now are. The same applies to you. Acknowledging that the role has gravitational pull is the first step to resisting it intentionally.
move.title: The Move
